title: Simon
description: Simon — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "Simon" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents for Simon, by J. Storer Clouston'

relations.label: IV: The Man from the West
XXII: Mr. Carrington and the Fiscal

memes.label: Alfred Cromarty
memes.description: Deceased brother of Sir Reginald Cromarty, believed to be the biological father of Cicely Farmond, with a reputation for a "gay" lifestyle.

memes.label: America
memes.description: The United States — particularly the American West — as a formative influence on Ned Cromarty's character and a symbolic counterpoint to Scottish social convention throughout the novel.

memes.label: Backstory & Inheritance
memes.description: Index of pages dealing with the novel's backstory — family histories, inheritances, past events that shape the present mystery, and the long shadow of the Rattar and Cromarty family legacies.

memes.label: Baronetcy
memes.description: A hereditary British title ranking below baron but above knight — the title held by Sir Reginald Cromarty, whose murder triggers the crisis of succession in the novel.

memes.label: Bicycle
memes.description: The bicycle serves as Carrington's signature mode of transport and a recurring motif — a democratic vehicle that moves the detective between town and country, linking the novel's social worlds and enabling chance encounters that propel the plot.

memes.label: Bisset's Assistant
memes.description: The investigative partnership formed at Keldale House between butler James Bisset and F. T. Carrington, who acted as Bisset's assistant in examining the Cromarty murder scene.

memes.label: Bisset's Resignation
memes.description: The resignation of James Bisset from Simon Rattar's household — a domestic event that signals the impostor's deteriorating control.

memes.label: Carrington's Investigation
memes.description: The independent investigation conducted by F. T. Carrington, a private enquiry agent from London, into the Cromarty murder — operating outside and in creative tension with the compromised official inquiry led by the impostor procurator fiscal.

memes.label: Carrington's Visit to Stanesland
memes.description: F. T. Carrington's strategic visit to Stanesland — an investigative interview with Ned Cromarty that established Ned's innocence, confirmed Cicely Farmond's character, and extracted crucial information about the £1,200 legacy from Lilian Cromarty's unguarded remarks.

memes.label: Castleknowe Farm
memes.description: A tenanted farm on the Cromarty estate whose letting to a Mr. Shearer — against Sir Reginald Cromarty's express instructions — became a flashpoint in the growing tension between the baronet and his factor Simon Rattar.

memes.label: All Chapters
memes.description: Complete index of all chapter pages in the Simon wiki with PN ranges, sorted by chapter number.

memes.label: Characters by Household
memes.description: Index of all characters in Simon grouped by their household affiliation — Keldale House, Stanesland, the Cromarty estate, and beyond.

memes.label: Charles Cromarty
memes.description: The previous baronet, whose death eighteen months before the novel's action set in motion the chain of inheritance that culminates in Sir Reginald's murder.

memes.label: Charles Cromarty's Death
memes.description: The death of the previous baronet eighteen months before the novel's action — the event that established the chain of inheritance culminating in Sir Reginald's murder.

memes.label: Cicely and Malcolm's Engagement
memes.description: The clandestine engagement between Cicely Farmond and Malcolm Cromarty — a romantic attachment that Sir Reginald Cromarty opposed on grounds of social eligibility, and which later became the basis for false suspicions of their involvement in the Cromarty murder.

memes.label: Cicely Farmond
memes.description: The young woman at the centre of the novel's romantic subplot — secretly engaged to Malcolm Cromarty and the object of Ned Cromarty's frank admiration.

memes.label: Cicely Farmond's Flight
memes.description: Cicely Farmond's sudden departure from Keldale by train, pursued by Ned Cromarty — a flight driven by the unbearable weight of suspicion and scandal, which became a turning point in their relationship.

memes.label: Cicely's Diaries
memes.description: The personal records, correspondence, and documentary evidence associated with Cicely Farmond, which played a role in establishing her identity and the events surrounding the Cromarty murder.

memes.label: All Concepts
memes.description: Complete index of all concept pages in the Simon wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Crime Scene & Forensics
memes.description: Pages related to the physical evidence, crime scene examination, and forensic details surrounding Simon Rattar's murder.

memes.label: Cromarty Estate
memes.description: The landed estate of the Cromarty family, comprising Keldale House and surrounding properties, managed by Simon Rattar as factor.

memes.label: Deduction
memes.description: The amateur detective methodology of James Bisset, whose "fair deductions" and logical reasoning in the Cromarty murder investigation constitute a recurring comic motif in the novel.

memes.label: Donald Mackay
memes.description: The elderly gardener on the Cromarty estate and one of the legatees in Sir Reginald Cromarty's will — bedridden with rheumatism at the time of the murder, placing him beyond suspicion despite his financial interest in the baronet's death.

memes.label: Engagement
memes.description: The formal promise of marriage — in the novel, engagements (both open and secret) drive romantic subplots and intersect with questions of inheritance and social standing.

memes.label: All Events
memes.description: Complete index of all event pages in the Simon wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Evidence
memes.description: The body of facts and testimony gathered during a criminal investigation — in the novel, the evidence against George Rattar builds through circumstantial detail, medical examination, and the deductions of a private enquiry agent until his confession.

memes.label: F. T. Carrington
memes.description: The London-based private enquiry agent whose independent investigation into the Cromarty murder uncovers the truth that the official fiscal is concealing.

memes.label: Family & Estate
memes.description: Thematic index of pages related to the Cromarty family, their estates, and the landed gentry world of Simon.

memes.label: Featured Content
memes.description: Statistical ranking of the most-cited pages in the Simon wiki, highlighting key topics and characters.

memes.label: "Firm of S. and G. Rattar"
memes.description: "The legal practice founded by Simon Rattar and originally including his brother George, serving as solicitors and factors to landowners in the county."

memes.label: Gentleman
memes.description: The ideal of gentlemanly conduct — combining social status, moral character, and behavioural codes — as portrayed and tested in the novel.

memes.label: George Rattar
memes.description: Simon Rattar's identical twin brother — a convicted criminal who murders Simon, assumes his identity, and impersonates him as procurator fiscal throughout the novel.

memes.label: "George Rattar Sr."
memes.description: "Father of Simon and George Rattar — the deceased patriarch whose legal firm and family legacy shaped the circumstances of the twin brothers' divergent fates."

memes.label: George Rattar's Confession
memes.description: The climactic confession in which George Rattar admits to murdering his twin brother and impersonating him — the resolution of the novel's central mystery.

memes.label: "George Rattar's Return"
memes.description: "The inciting event of the novel — George Rattar's secret return to his brother Simon's villa after years of absence, setting in motion the murders, the impersonation, and the unraveling of the Cromarty mystery."

memes.label: Glass Eye
memes.description: Ned Cromarty's prosthetic eye — a distinctive physical feature with a violent backstory that serves as both a character marker and a recurring motif in the novel.

memes.label: Glass Match-Box
memes.description: The massive globular glass match-box from Simon Rattar's library — an apparently innocuous domestic object used as the murder weapon in the Cromarty killing.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: The moral code governing gentlemanly behaviour in Edwardian society — a standard invoked, questioned, and subverted throughout the novel.

memes.label: Identity & Deception
memes.description: Thematic index of pages related to themes of identity, mistaken identity, deception, and doubles in Simon.

memes.label: "Identity Theft"
memes.description: "The central crime of the novel — George Rattar's theft of his murdered twin brother Simon's identity, profession, and social position, sustained over several weeks."

memes.label: Imposture & Disguise
memes.description: Pages related to physical disguise, impersonation, and the theme of imposture in J. Storer Clouston's Simon — where who someone appears to be is not always who they are.

memes.label: Investigation Topics
memes.description: Thematic index of pages related to the investigation of Simon Rattar's murder, including detectives, methods, and procedures.

memes.label: James Bisset
memes.description: Simon Rattar's butler, a self-styled philosopher whose domestic position and observational habits make him an informal detective within the Rattar household, and whose evidence proves crucial to the murder investigation.

memes.label: James Ison
memes.description: The head clerk in the firm of S. and G. Rattar, whose long service and intimate knowledge of the legal practice make him a witness to the impostor's irregularities.

memes.label: Janet the Cook
memes.description: Simon Rattar's cook at his villa, roommate of Mary MacLean, whose cheerful skepticism and sharp tongue provide a grounding counterpoint to the household's growing tension — and whose kitchen becomes an unwitting stage for Carrington's detective stratagem.

memes.label: "John Robertson"
memes.description: "Sir Reginald Cromarty's chauffeur — a minor legatee excluded from suspicion by a verified alibi on the night of the murder."

memes.label: Keldale House
memes.description: The country seat of the Cromarty family — a Scottish estate of lodge gates and sweeping avenue, the site of Sir Reginald Cromarty's murder, and the symbolic centre of the novel's social and moral order.

memes.label: Kid Gloves
memes.description: An old pair of kid gloves kept by Simon Rattar in his office desk, worn when alone to avoid leaving fingerprints — a crucial piece of evidence revealing George Rattar's identity swap.

memes.label: Kings Arms Hotel
memes.description: The principal hotel in the unnamed Scottish town — Carrington's base of operations and a key site for gathering local intelligence.

memes.label: Kings Arms Hotel (establishment)
memes.description: The principal hotel and commercial establishment in the town, managed by Miss Peterkin, serving as a central gathering place and base of operations for several characters.

memes.label: Kirk Elder
memes.description: The office of elder in the Church of Scotland (the Kirk), a position of religious and community leadership held in the novel by Simon Rattar, reflecting the intersection of legal, social, and ecclesiastical authority in Scottish parish life.

memes.label: Lady Cromarty
memes.description: The wife of Sir Reginald Cromarty and chatelaine of Keldale House — a woman of social standing and quiet authority whose domestic world is shattered by her husband's murder, and who becomes a key figure in the investigation and its resolution.

memes.label: Laird
memes.description: A Scottish landowner holding a lairdship — a title and social position central to the identity of Ned Cromarty in the novel.

memes.label: Lawyer
memes.description: The legal profession as portrayed in the novel — embodied primarily by Simon Rattar, whose dual role as lawyer and factor places him at the intersection of law, land, and local power in a Scottish county.

memes.label: Legal & Procedure
memes.description: Index of pages related to legal proceedings, criminal investigation, evidence, and courtroom matters in the Simon wiki.

memes.label: Letter
memes.description: Written correspondence as a plot mechanism — letters in the novel function as evidence, confession, and dramatic revelation, most notably in Chapter X: "The Letter."

memes.label: Lilian Cromarty
memes.description: Ned Cromarty's sister and hostess at Stanesland, whose domestic stability anchors her unconventional brother and whose own emotional life is tested by the events surrounding the murder investigation.

memes.label: Malcolm Cromarty
memes.description: The heir to the Cromarty baronetcy, a young man of fashion secretly engaged to Cicely Farmond, whose inheritance prospects are transformed by Sir Reginald's murder and whose character is tested by suspicion.

memes.label: Mary MacLean
memes.description: The housemaid in Simon Rattar's household whose discovery of a mysterious stranger in the fiscal's garden initiates the investigation, and whose emotional perceptiveness makes her an unwitting detector of the truth beneath the imposture.

memes.label: Miss Peterkin
memes.description: The manageress of the Kings Arms Hotel, whose local knowledge and conversational availability make her a valuable source of information for Carrington.

memes.label: Motive
memes.description: The driving reason behind a crime — in the novel, the search for motive structures the investigation into Sir Reginald Cromarty's murder and reveals the hidden pressures of inheritance, family, and reputation.

memes.label: Murder
memes.description: "The act of unlawful killing that lies at the heart of the novel's plot — the murder of Sir Reginald Cromarty shapes the story's mystery, its investigation, and its moral universe."

memes.label: Murder & Mystery
memes.description: Thematic index of pages related to the murder at the heart of Simon — the crime, suspects, and resolution.

memes.label: Ned and Cicely's Engagement
memes.description: The romantic resolution of the novel — Ned Cromarty's successful courtship of Cicely Farmond, contrasting with the secrecy of her previous engagement to Malcolm.

memes.label: Ned Cromarty
memes.description: The laird of Stanesland, an ex-cowpuncher returned from America whose glass eye and frontier directness mark him as the novel's unconventional hero.

memes.label: Ned Cromarty's Cowboy Past
memes.description: Ned Cromarty's formative years spent in the wilder parts of America — a period of adventure and hard living that shaped his unconventional character and set him apart from the expectations of Scottish landed society.

memes.label: Ned Cromarty's Return
memes.description: Ned Cromarty's return from America to inherit Stanesland — the event that brings the novel's most unconventional character back into Scottish county society and sets the stage for his entanglement in the Cromarty murder investigation.

memes.label: All Organizations
memes.description: Complete index of all organization pages in the Simon wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: All Persons
memes.description: Complete index of all person pages in the Simon wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Philosopher
memes.description: The identity assumed by James Bisset, butler at Keldale House — a servant whose philosophical detachment and habits of observation make him an unexpected ally in Carrington's investigation and a distinctive narrative voice in the novel.

memes.label: All Places
memes.description: Complete index of all place pages in the Simon wiki, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Procurator Fiscal
memes.description: The public prosecutor in Scotland responsible for investigating sudden deaths and initiating criminal proceedings — the office held by Simon Rattar in the novel, whose authority becomes a weapon in the hands of his impostor.

memes.label: "Procurator Fiscal's Office"
memes.description: "The office of the Procurator Fiscal in the unnamed Scottish county town, held by Simon Rattar, responsible for setting criminal processes in motion and directing the police."

memes.label: The Rattar Family
memes.description: The family of Simon and George Rattar, whose complex relationship — shaped by rivalry, financial pressure, and ultimately murder — forms one of the central conflicts of the novel.

memes.label: Rattar's Office
memes.description: The law office of the firm of S. and G. Rattar — the professional domain of the procurator fiscal and the setting for key client consultations.
aliases: Firm of S. and G. Rattar

memes.label: "Rattar's Signet Ring"
memes.description: "Simon Rattar's gold signet ring — crucial physical evidence that could not be removed from his corpse, becoming the decisive proof of his identity and George's crime."

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: The public estimation of a person's character — in the novel, reputation functions as both social currency and a mask that conceals criminal reality.

memes.label: Robert MacAlister
memes.description: Superintendent Sutherland's cousin, a local resident whose "intelligent, inveterate and persevering curiosity" makes him both an accidental witness to the muffled stranger on the railway platform and an unwitting saboteur of the investigation — his eagerness to help leading directly to the suspect's escape.

memes.label: Romance & Courtship
memes.description: Pages covering the romantic subplots in Simon — engagements, secret courtships, and the marriage prospects that drive the novel's personal drama.

memes.label: "Sandy Donaldson"
memes.description: "The town drunkard of the Kings Arms Hotel — a garrulous local whose loose talk provided Carrington with clues and misdirection in equal measure."

memes.label: Scottish Legal System
memes.description: The distinctive legal institutions of Scotland — Procurator Fiscal, Sheriff Court, and local investigation practices — that form the institutional backdrop of the Cromarty murder investigation, and whose procedures are both followed and exploited by the killer.

memes.label: Secret
memes.description: Information deliberately concealed — in the novel, secrets drive the plot through clandestine engagements, hidden identities, and suppressed evidence.

memes.label: Servant
memes.description: Domestic servants in early 20th-century Scottish households — figures who function as observers, witnesses, and agents within the novel's social hierarchy.

memes.label: Servants & Staff
memes.description: Thematic index of servant and staff characters who appear in Simon — cooks, chauffeurs, gardeners, and household workers.

memes.label: Settings & Locations
memes.description: Index of pages describing the physical settings and locations in the Simon wiki — from Keldale House to Stanesland, from the Kings Arms to the Cromarty estate.

memes.label: Silent Simon
memes.description: The nickname given to Simon Rattar, reflecting his taciturn manner, inscrutable demeanour, and the public reputation that concealed his private reality.

memes.label: Simon Rattar
memes.description: The procurator fiscal and lawyer of an unnamed northern Scottish county — a man of formidable professional reputation whose murder and replacement by his twin brother George is the central crime of the novel. Known as "Silent Simon" for his taciturn manner, he embodies the concentrated power of law, land, and local authority in rural Scotland.

memes.label: "Simon Rattar's Burial"
memes.description: "George Rattar's secret burial of his murdered twin brother Simon's body in a flower bed in the garden of Simon Rattar's Villa — the act of concealment that lay at the heart of the novel's central mystery."

memes.label: Simon Rattar's Villa
memes.description: The villa residence of Simon Rattar — setting for the imposture, site of the original murder, and the domestic stage on which the impersonation is performed and eventually exposed.

memes.label: Sir Reginald Cromarty
memes.description: The benevolent baronet of Keldale House whose murder in his library sets the novel's central mystery in motion.

memes.label: Smoking as Identity
memes.description: The smoking habit — or its absence — as a distinguishing marker of identity in the novel, where Simon Rattar's principled non-smoking became a key clue separating him from his smoking brother George, and a forensic detail that helped unravel the impersonation.

memes.label: Stanesland
memes.description: The Cromarty family's coastal estate — an old castle kept by Ned Cromarty, the laird of Stanesland, and his sister Lilian. A place of refuge and authenticity set against the formal world of Keldale House.

memes.label: Superintendent Sutherland
memes.description: The local police superintendent tasked with investigating the Cromarty murder under the direction of the impostor fiscal.

memes.label: Suspicion
memes.description: The corrosive atmosphere of doubt and mistrust that pervades the novel — suspicion falls on nearly every character, shaping relationships, driving the investigation, and testing the bonds of family and friendship.

memes.label: The Arrest of Sir Malcolm Cromarty
memes.description: The arrest of Sir Malcolm Cromarty on suspicion of murdering his father Sir Reginald — a dramatic turn in the Cromarty murder investigation that briefly redirected suspicion away from Ned Cromarty but proved to be unfounded.

memes.label: "The Burn"
memes.description: "A small stream near Keldale House, running beside a footpath bordered by willows — the scene of a carefully staged encounter between Carrington and a local figure."

memes.label: The Confrontation with the Prisoner
memes.description: The climactic confrontation at Simon Rattar's villa in which Ned Cromarty faced the impostor procurator fiscal while Carrington and Superintendent Sutherland prepared the arrest — culminating in the revelation that "Simon Rattar" was in fact his twin brother George.

memes.label: The County Sheriff
memes.description: The Scottish legal office of procurator fiscal (colloquially "Shirra"), the county official responsible for initiating criminal proceedings and directing police investigations, held in the novel by Simon Rattar.

memes.label: The Cromarty Family
memes.description: The oldest branch of the Cromarty family, central to the novel's plot through Sir Reginald's murder, the estate inheritance, and the family's legal affairs — managed for generations by the firm of S. and G. Rattar.

relations.label: firm of S. and G. Rattar

memes.label: The Cromarty Inheritance
memes.description: The legal and financial disposition of the Cromarty baronetcy and estate following the deaths of Charles Cromarty and Sir Reginald Cromarty — a question of succession that generates motive, suspicion, and narrative tension throughout the novel.

memes.label: The Cromarty Murder
memes.description: The murder of Sir Reginald Cromarty in his library at Keldale House — the central crime that drives the novel's plot, triggers twin investigations, and exposes the larger crime of impersonation at the heart of the county's legal establishment.
aliases: The Murder of Sir Reginald Cromarty

memes.label: The Discovery in the Garden
memes.description: The discovery and excavation of Simon Rattar's body from the flower bed of his own villa — the physical evidence that enabled Carrington to arrest "Simon Rattar" as the impostor George Rattar.
aliases: the excavation of Simon Rattar's body

memes.label: The Forged Will
memes.description: The will of Sir Reginald Cromarty — read by Simon Rattar in his capacity as factor and scrutinised by Carrington as the investigation unfolded — which became a central document in establishing the financial motives behind the murder.

memes.label: The Hearth Brush
memes.description: A critical piece of physical evidence in the Cromarty murder investigation — mud fragments swept up with the hearth brush and placed in an unusual position, analyzed by James Bisset and contested by Simon Rattar.

relations.label: the murder of Sir Reginald Cromarty

memes.label: The Impersonation
memes.description: George Rattar's murder of his twin brother Simon and subsequent assumption of his identity as procurator fiscal — the novel's foundational crime.

memes.label: "The Kirk"
memes.description: "The Church of Scotland (the Kirk) — the national church and a significant institutional presence in the community, of which Simon Rattar was an elder."

memes.label: The Letter (Chapter X)
memes.description: The provocation and dispatch of a business letter from Sir Reginald Cromarty to Simon Rattar over the letting of Castleknowe Farm — a seemingly minor dispute that brought the baronet into direct conflict with his procurator fiscal on the very day of the murder.

memes.label: The Middle Blind
memes.description: A broken window blind at Keldale House — the middle blind of the library window — that became a key piece of evidence in the Cromarty murder investigation, indicating that someone had manipulated the window coverings.

memes.label: The Moorlands
memes.description: The open moorland surrounding the unnamed Scottish town — a space of isolation and darkness that contrasts with the constrained interiors of the novel's domestic and professional settings.

memes.label: The Murder of Simon Rattar
memes.description: The murder of Simon Rattar by his twin brother George — the originating crime that precedes and enables the impersonation, and the act that the novel's investigation ultimately unravels.

memes.label: "The Railway Incident"
memes.description: "The night Superintendent Sutherland encountered a mysterious muffled figure on a train — a man who vanished from his compartment before Sutherland could investigate — later understood to be George Rattar eluding the police."

memes.label: The Railway Station
memes.description: The railway station serving the unnamed Scottish town — a point of arrival and departure that marks characters' entrances into and exits from the narrative, and the setting of the novel's opening scene and the "empty compartment" mystery.

memes.label: The Reading of Simon Rattar's Will
memes.description: The disclosure of Sir Reginald Cromarty's will by the impostor procurator fiscal — a calculated revelation of beneficiaries and legacies that shaped the investigation's theory of motive and cast suspicion on several members of the Cromarty household.

memes.label: The Secret Engagement
memes.description: The clandestine engagement between Ned Cromarty and Cicely Farmond — a romantic attachment conducted in secrecy because of Sir Reginald Cromarty's known opposition to the match.

memes.label: The Sympathetic Stranger
memes.description: A mysterious figure who appears at multiple key moments in the investigation, presenting himself as a friendly outsider with a keen interest in the Keldale House murder — later revealed to be F. T. Carrington in disguise.

memes.label: The Town
memes.description: The unnamed northern Scottish county town where the novel is set — the stage on which its drama of murder, imposture, and investigation unfolds.

memes.label: The Walking Stick
memes.description: A stout walking stick carried by F. T. Carrington — both a gentleman's accessory and an instrument of detection, used to probe flower beds, test surfaces, and ultimately uncover the physical evidence that confirms the identity of the murderer.

memes.label: The Walking Stick Discovery
memes.description: F. T. Carrington's covert investigation of Simon Rattar's garden using a specially purchased walking stick — the breakthrough that confirmed the presence of a buried body and provided the physical evidence needed to arrest the impostor procurator fiscal.

memes.label: Twin Resemblance
memes.description: The extraordinary physical similarity between Simon and George Rattar, identical twins whose indistinguishable appearance enables George to murder Simon and assume his identity undetected.

memes.label: Whisky
memes.description: Scotch whisky as a social lubricant, cultural symbol, and narrative device — present in key scenes from Ned Cromarty's cowboy past to the Kings Arms Hotel.

memes.label: Witnesses & Testimony
memes.description: Index of witness characters who observed key events or testified during the investigation in J. Storer Clouston's Simon.
